text: The surgeon inserts a varus needle into the umbilicus to establish pneumoperitoneum, followed by the placement of a 10mm trocar below the xiphoid to the left of midline. Under direct visualization, five additional trocars are placed: 5mm and 12mm trocars in the left upper quadrant, a 5mm port in the subxiphoid space, and 5mm and 12mm trocars in the right upper quadrant.

During exploration, the surgeon identifies a retrosternal diaphragmatic defect with herniation of the entire small bowel, omentum, and transverse colon. The herniated contents appear incarcerated and irreducible. Dissection begins with blunt traction on the right side of the defect, dividing adhesions and removing the falciform ligament using traction and ligature. The anterior and posterior portions of the hernia sac are mobilized, allowing for complete reduction of the hernia contents.

The surgeon measures the diaphragmatic defect at approximately ten by six centimeters. A Jackson Pratt drain is placed in the right lower thoracic cavity and exited through the five millimeter subxiphoid port. The surrounding tissue is cleaned of debris, and a ten centimeter by fifteen centimeter polyester parietex composite mesh is sutured circumferentially with number one proline to repair the defect.
